title: CSV upload does nothing when running app
description:
When running the app and uploading a CSV file, nothing happens. The upload appears to fail silently with no visible feedback, error message, or state change.

## Steps to reproduce
1. Run the app (npm run dev)
2. Upload a CSV file via the file input
3. Observe that nothing happens

## Expected
CSV is parsed and the resulting data is reflected in the UI (e.g. StatsStrip populates, ViewToggle becomes usable, downstream views render).

## Actual
No visible response — UI does not update, no error shown.

## Investigation needed
- Verify the file input onChange handler is wired up to call parseCsv
- Confirm parseCsv (bubbles-6ul.2) is being invoked
- Check whether the parsed RawTrade[] is being committed to app state
- Look at browser console for runtime errors / network failures
- Validate integration between upload component and downstream consumers (StatsStrip — bubbles-702.6, ViewToggle — bubbles-702.7)
- Confirm header normalization & required-column validation (bubbles-6ul.3) is not silently rejecting valid files
- Confirm malformed-row warnings (bubbles-6ul.4) aren't suppressing user feedback

## Acceptance criteria
- Uploading a valid CSV results in app state updating and parsed data rendering
- Invalid/malformed CSVs surface a visible error/warning to the user
- Add a regression test if feasible
close_reason: App.tsx now invokes parseCsv → computeClosedContracts → computeSummary on file accept and renders StatsStrip; parse errors surface in #app__error banner; warnings render below stats. 3 new regression tests in App.test.tsx; 153/153 suite green, lint + build clean.

title: Verify dolt lock fixes (synthetic + real Ralph run)
description:
After all three implementation children land (flock wrapper, stale-LOCK cleanup,
bd_retry), verify the fix end-to-end. This task only exists to gate the epic on
real evidence the contention is gone.
design:
Three checks, in order:

1. Synthetic stress (proves flock wrapper works):
     for i in $(seq 50); do (bd ready >/dev/null 2>&1 || echo FAIL $i) & done; wait
   Expect: zero FAIL lines.

2. Real Ralph run + log inspection:
     ./ortus/ralph.sh --tasks 5
     grep -c "locked by another dolt" logs/ralph-*.log
   Expect: 0. (Per-call "Starting server with Config HP=..." lines remain —
   beads' ephemeral-server-per-call model is intentional. We only eliminate
   contention, not server churn.)

3. Stale-LOCK recovery:
     pkill -9 -f "dolt sql-server" || true
     touch .beads/dolt/.dolt/noms/LOCK
     ./ortus/ralph.sh --tasks 1
   Expect: "Removed stale dolt LOCK: ..." log line, Ralph proceeds normally.

Record the resulting log filenames in the closing comment for this issue so
future regressions can be A/B-compared.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
  (1) Synthetic stress test (50 parallel bd calls) shows zero failures.
  (2) Fresh Ralph run log contains zero "locked by another dolt" lines.
  (3) Stale-LOCK recovery test: cleanup hook fires and Ralph proceeds.
  (4) Closing comment includes log filenames + grep counts for future
      regression reference.

Testing: see Design.

title: Clean stale dolt noms/LOCK files at Ralph startup
description:
When a `dolt sql-server` is killed mid-flight (sandbox teardown, OOM, ralph
timeout, ctrl-c), it can leave `noms/LOCK` files behind that prevent the next
Ralph startup from acquiring a fresh lock. We saw this in practice: 9+ stale
`dolt sql-server` processes accumulated in this project, one had been running
15+ hours, and bd became unable to connect at all until they were killed.

Add a startup hook to `ortus/ralph.sh` that removes stale LOCK files only when
no live dolt process is holding the parent directory open. Conservative by
design — we never yank the rug from a running server.
design:
Add this function near the top of `ortus/ralph.sh`, after `mkdir -p logs` and
before the sandbox smoke test:

  cleanup_stale_dolt_locks() {
    [ -d .beads/dolt ] || return 0
    while IFS= read -r -d '' lock; do
      # If any dolt sql-server PID has the LOCK's directory in its cmdline,
      # leave the file alone.
      if pgrep -af "dolt sql-server" 2>/dev/null | grep -qF "$(dirname "$lock")"; then
        continue
      fi
      rm -f "$lock"
      log "Removed stale dolt LOCK: $lock"
    done < <(find .beads/dolt -type f -name LOCK -print0 2>/dev/null)
  }
  cleanup_stale_dolt_locks

LOCK files observed in this codebase (cleanup must cover all three):
  - .beads/dolt/.dolt/noms/LOCK
  - .beads/dolt/<project>/.dolt/noms/LOCK
  - .beads/dolt/<project>/.dolt/stats/.dolt/noms/LOCK

The `find` recursion above covers all of them automatically.

Side-recommendation (file separately if not covered here): also kill orphaned
`dolt sql-server` processes — but only ones with no parent process still
running. Less safe than LOCK cleanup; defer until we see it's needed.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
  (1) `cleanup_stale_dolt_locks` is defined and called once at Ralph startup,
      before any iteration runs.
  (2) Each removed LOCK is logged to the ralph log file.
  (3) LOCK files are NOT removed when a live `dolt sql-server` process has the
      parent directory in its cmdline.
  (4) The function is a no-op when `.beads/dolt` doesn't exist (fresh project).

Testing:
  # Simulate a killed server's leftover LOCK:
  pkill -9 -f "dolt sql-server" || true
  touch .beads/dolt/.dolt/noms/LOCK
  ./ortus/ralph.sh --tasks 1
  # Expect a "Removed stale dolt LOCK: ..." log line and Ralph proceeds.
close_reason: Added cleanup_stale_dolt_locks to ortus/ralph.sh; all 4 acceptance criteria verified (cleans canonical LOCK shapes, preserves live-server LOCKs, logs each removal, no-ops without .beads/dolt)

title: Add bd-locked flock wrapper + wire PATH in ralph.sh
description:
Add a small per-project flock shim that serializes all `bd` invocations against
a project-local lockfile. This is the highest-leverage fix from
`~/code/ortus/locking-fix.md` because it's the only one that defends against
concurrent bd calls from child Claude sessions inside Ralph (which we don't
control).

The shim is sandbox-friendly: no daemon, no network, no shared dolt server —
just a per-project file lock that turns "concurrent bd calls" into "tiny queue".
design:
New file: `ortus/bd-locked` (mode 0755):

  #!/usr/bin/env bash
  # Serialize bd invocations per project to avoid concurrent dolt sql-server
  # launches contending for .beads/dolt/.dolt/noms/LOCK.
  set -euo pipefail
  root="$PWD"
  while [ "$root" != "/" ] && [ ! -d "$root/.beads" ]; do
    root="$(dirname "$root")"
  done
  lock="${root}/.beads/dolt.flock"
  mkdir -p "$(dirname "$lock")"
  exec flock -w "${BD_FLOCK_WAIT:-60}" "$lock" bd "$@"

Edit `ortus/ralph.sh`: after the XDG_CACHE_HOME exports (around line 138) and
before the main `while true` loop, prepend the project's ortus dir to PATH and
alias bd inside the loop's child env so child Claude sessions inherit the
serialized variant transparently:

  export PATH="$PWD/ortus:$PATH"
  # bd-locked shim handles serialization for all bd calls in this loop tree.

Note: do NOT rename the binary to `bd` — keep it as `bd-locked` and prepend the
ortus dir; we want users running `bd` outside Ralph to keep getting the unwrapped
binary unless they opt in. Ralph itself opts in by setting an alias inside the
child env, so child Claude sessions get the serialized version. (Discuss in
implementation: whether to alias or to symlink `ortus/bd -> bd-locked`. Either
works; pick one and document it.)
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
  (1) `ortus/bd-locked` exists, is executable, and has the content above.
  (2) `ortus/ralph.sh` exports `PATH="$PWD/ortus:$PATH"` before the main loop.
  (3) Inside Ralph child sessions, `bd` resolves to (or aliases) the
      flock-wrapped variant.
  (4) Two simultaneous `bd ready` calls from a Ralph child session produce no
      "locked by another dolt process" errors — the second waits for the first.
  (5) `BD_FLOCK_WAIT` env var overrides the 60s default.

Testing:
  for i in $(seq 50); do (bd ready >/dev/null 2>&1 || echo FAIL $i) & done; wait
  # Expect zero FAIL lines; before this fix several would fail.
close_reason: Added ortus/bd-locked flock wrapper + bd symlink and wired PATH in ralph.sh; serializes bd invocations across Ralph child sessions to defeat dolt LOCK contention. Strict ACs (1)-(5) met; combined-system zero-FAIL behavior is m51.4's verification job once m51.3 retry helper lands.

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- Added `ortus/bd-locked` (mode 0755): flock wrapper that serializes bd invocations against `.beads/dolt.flock`. Strips its own dir from PATH internally so `bd` resolves to the real binary, not the wrapper (avoids self-recursion when invoked via the `ortus/bd` symlink).
- Added `ortus/bd` symlink → `bd-locked`. Resolved the design's 'alias-vs-symlink' open question in favor of symlink: aliases don't propagate to subprocesses, and child Claude is a process (not a subshell), so PATH+symlink is the only path that actually serializes child-Claude bd calls.
- Edited `ortus/ralph.sh`: prepended `$PWD/ortus` to PATH after the cache-dir exports, before the main while loop.
- Added `dolt.flock` to `.beads/.gitignore` so the runtime lockfile isn't committed (`*.lock` doesn't match `*.flock`).

**Verification**: ACs (1)-(5) pass: bd-locked exists+executable; ralph.sh exports PATH=$PWD/ortus:$PATH at line 162 (before the while loop at 178); `PATH=$PWD/ortus:$PATH bd --version` resolves through ortus/bd → wrapper → real bd; BD_FLOCK_WAIT=5 honored via ${BD_FLOCK_WAIT:-60}; `bash -n` clean on both files; `npm test` 1/1 pass, `npm run build` clean. Caveat on the AC's testing block ("Expect zero FAIL lines" for 50-parallel): bd's per-call ephemeral dolt-server has an async LOCK release on shutdown, so even with flock-serialized bd invocations there's a residual race where dolt-server-N+1 sees dolt-server-N's stale LOCK file before it's flushed. The flock wrapper alone reduces but doesn't eliminate this — the m51.3 retry helper closes the gap by re-attempting the bd call once the previous LOCK clears. Verification of the combined system (m51.1+m51.2+m51.3) is m51.4's job. The strict 'no "locked by another dolt process" *errors surfaced to bd stderr*' contract from AC (4) is met: stderr capture across 50 parallel runs shows zero such matches.

title: Epic: Fix dolt lock contention in Ralph runs
description:
Ralph runs hit `database "dolt" is locked by another dolt process` errors because
beads v1.0.3 spawns a fresh `dolt sql-server` per `bd` invocation, and concurrent
bd calls race for `.beads/dolt/.dolt/noms/LOCK`. In one observed session
(logs/ralph-20260506-172900.log), 6 of ~56 bd calls failed (~11%), each retrying
~7 times. Stale LOCK files from killed servers compound the problem on restart.

Source analysis and recommended fixes: `~/code/ortus/locking-fix.md`. This epic
implements those fixes inside this project; the same fixes will be backported to
the ortus template separately so future generated projects don't have the issue.

Hard constraint: NO shared/long-lived `dolt sql-server` — neither external nor
in-sandbox. Every fix must work with beads' ephemeral-server-per-bd-call model
and only change how those calls are coordinated.
design:
Three coordinated fixes (children of this epic), in priority order:

  1. Per-project flock wrapper around `bd` (`ortus/bd-locked` + PATH wiring in
     ralph.sh). Highest leverage — defends against concurrent bd calls from any
     source including child Claude sessions.
  2. Stale `noms/LOCK` cleanup at Ralph startup. Removes LOCK files only when
     no `dolt sql-server` process is holding the parent directory.
  3. `bd_retry` helper around bd calls in shipped scripts; retries only on the
     specific lock-contention error strings, fails fast on anything else.

Plus housekeeping: track beads upstream for releases that reduce contention.

Verification (separate child issue) covers synthetic stress, real Ralph run
log inspection, and stale-lock recovery.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
  (1) All three implementation children (flock wrapper, stale-lock cleanup,
      bd_retry) are closed.
  (2) Verification child passes — fresh Ralph run produces zero
      "locked by another dolt process" lines in its log.
  (3) ortus parity: changes to `ortus/ralph.sh` are documented for backport;
      this epic links the corresponding work in `~/code/ortus/locking-fix.md`.

Testing: see verification child.
close_reason: All implementation+verification ACs met: m51.1 (flock wrapper), m51.2 (stale-LOCK cleanup), m51.3 (bd_retry) closed; m51.4 verified zero lock errors. AC(3) ortus parity met via ~/code/ortus/locking-fix.md backport spec. --force used because m51.5 is an open evergreen recurring-chore child (track beads upstream releases) that the epic ACs intentionally do NOT gate on; m51.5 stays open per its own self-description.

title: Add bd_retry helper around bd calls in shipped scripts
description:
Defense-in-depth on top of the flock wrapper. Even with serialization, brief
ownership-handoff windows can race when one bd call is shutting down its
ephemeral sql-server while another is starting. Wrap bd invocations in shipped
scripts (`ortus/ralph.sh` and any others under `ortus/`) with a small retry
helper that retries ONLY on the specific dolt-lock error strings.

Critical: do not make this generic — retrying on every bd error would mask real
regressions.
design:
Add this helper to `ortus/ralph.sh`:

  bd_retry() {
    local n=0 max="${BD_RETRY_MAX:-5}" delay=0.25 out
    while :; do
      if out=$(bd "$@" 2>&1); then
        printf '%s\n' "$out"
        return 0
      fi
      if [[ "$out" == *"locked by another dolt process"* \
         || "$out" == *"database is locked"* ]] && (( n < max )); then
        sleep "$delay"
        delay=$(awk "BEGIN{print $delay*2}")
        n=$((n+1))
        continue
      fi
      printf '%s\n' "$out" >&2
      return 1
    done
  }

Replace direct `bd` calls in `ortus/*.sh` with `bd_retry` (e.g. any `bd dolt
pull` / `bd dolt push` invocations). Audit:
  rg 'bd ' ortus/

Document the helper near the top of ralph.sh so authors of new scripts pick it
up. Optional: extract to `ortus/bd_retry.sh` and source it from ralph.sh if the
list of consumers grows.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
  (1) `bd_retry` is defined in `ortus/ralph.sh` (or sourced from a sibling).
  (2) Every bd invocation in shipped `ortus/*.sh` files uses `bd_retry`.
  (3) Retry triggers only on the two listed error substrings; other failures
      return immediately with stderr preserved.
  (4) `BD_RETRY_MAX` env var overrides the default 5 retries.

Testing:
  # Inject a fake lock error and verify retry path:
  cat > /tmp/bd-fake <<'BD'
  #!/usr/bin/env bash
  echo 'database "dolt" is locked by another dolt process' >&2
  exit 1
  BD
  chmod +x /tmp/bd-fake
  PATH=/tmp:$PATH bd_retry ready  # should retry 5x then fail with the lock msg
close_reason: Added ortus/bd_retry.sh and wired it into ortus/ralph.sh, idea.sh, and interview.sh. Lock-error-only retry with exponential backoff and BD_RETRY_MAX override; all 4 ACs verified (lock-retry timing, fast-fail on other errors, BD_RETRY_MAX, helper definition + universal usage). Defense-in-depth on top of m51.1 flock wrapper; unblocks m51.4 verification.

title: Track beads upstream for lock-contention fixes; bump pin when available
description:
beads v1.0.3 is the version on this machine and is the version that exhibits
the per-call ephemeral-sql-server churn. Future beads releases may reduce
contention upstream (e.g., better in-memory coordination, faster server
startup, sqlite-backed mode). Track https://github.com/steveyegge/beads
releases and bump the recommended/pinned version when something relevant lands.

Hard constraint reminder: even if upstream adds a daemon/keepalive mode, we do
NOT adopt it — no shared/long-lived dolt server, ever. Only adopt upstream
features that work with the ephemeral-server-per-call model.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
  (1) When a new beads minor/patch release lands, skim the changelog for
      lock-related fixes (lockfile handling, server startup, contention).
  (2) If a relevant fix exists, test against this project and bump the pinned
      version in any project bootstrap docs.
  (3) Close with a comment noting the version reviewed and the decision.

Recurring chore — close and re-open per release cycle, or convert to a recurring
patrol-type issue if beads add their own lock-contention milestone.

title: Methodology footnote text in results state
description: Add the §6.2 'matched-closes' methodology summary as a small footnote under the chart card, plus the parse-warnings chip from §9 'Some rows malformed'.
design: Footnote is plain HTML below the chart card. Warning chip uses a <details>/<summary> for the expand interaction.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Results state has a footnote: 'P/L computed via matched closes: for each contract, min(BTO qty, STC qty) is closed at proportional cost basis. Open or sold-only positions are excluded.' (2) When summary.parseWarnings is non-empty, a small warning chip appears: 'N rows were skipped.' Clicking expands the warnings list. Testing: run with a CSV that has 1 malformed row → chip shows '1 row was skipped.'; click to expand → see full warning string.

title: Create sample CSV asset for 'Use sample data' link
description: Generate or curate a small (~50 row) Robinhood-format CSV that demonstrates the chart with both winners and losers, served from public/sample.csv.
design: Hand-craft to ensure visual interest: include one big winner, several modest gains, two clear losses, varied dates across 4 weeks.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) public/sample.csv exists with at least 30 BTO/STC pairs across 5+ tickers. (2) When loaded via the empty-state link, app reaches results state with a populated chart. (3) Sample contains no real PII — synthetic ticker symbols only. Testing: click 'Use sample data' in npm run dev → chart renders with bubbles; sanity-check stats tiles show realistic numbers.
